column1: What is the recommended S3 lifecycle policy for managing data that becomes less frequently accessed over time?
column2: Move data to S3 Infrequent Access after 30 days, then to Glacier after 90 days for cost optimization

column1: What type of storage does Amazon S3 provide?
column2: Object storage (not block storage)

column1: How are S3 objects identified?
column2: By a URL generated from the bucket name, service region endpoint, and key name

column1: What happens when you restore an EBS volume from a snapshot?
column2: The volume is created immediately and data is loaded lazily (accessible upon creation, data restored on first request)

column1: Does an EBS-optimized instance affect the time to restore a volume?
column2: No, it provides dedicated capacity for EBS I/O but doesn't affect restoration time

column1: Can S3 be natively mounted as a file system?
column2: No, mounting S3 as a file system is not native functionality and requires additional software

column1: Does S3 bucket storage need to be preallocated?
column2: No, unlike EBS volumes, S3 storage can grow in a virtually unlimited manner

column1: What type of data is Amazon S3 Glacier optimized for?
column2: Long-term archival storage (not suited for immediate access or data deleted within 90 days)

column1: What S3 feature allows time-limited permission to download objects?
column2: Presigned URLs grant time-limited download permissions

column1: Can AWS IAM policies control access for authenticated users of a web application?
column2: No, IAM policies don't know who authenticated web app users are (they're not IAM users)

column1: Do EBS volumes automatically encrypt data at creation?
column2: Yes, EBS volumes are automatically encrypted at creation

column1: Do EBS volumes persist when an EC2 instance is stopped?
column2: Yes, EBS volumes persist when the instance is stopped

column1: What EFS performance mode is optimized for concurrent access from many EC2 instances?
column2: Max I/O performance mode (for tens, hundreds, or thousands of instances with higher aggregate throughput)

column1: What is the default EFS performance mode?
column2: General-Purpose performance mode (appropriate for most file systems)

column1: Does S3 support FTP transfers?
column2: No, S3 does not support FTP transfers

column1: What type of EBS volume is best for short periods of heavy traffic with long idle times?
column2: General-purpose SSD volumes with bursting capability (builds IOPS credits during idle time)

column1: Can objects be uploaded directly into S3 Glacier tiers?
column2: Yes, objects may be uploaded directly into Glacier tiers

column1: Do all S3 Glacier tiers have retrieval fees?
column2: Yes, all Glacier tiers have some fee for retrieval

column1: How many EC2 instances can connect to an Amazon EFS file system concurrently?
column2: One to thousands of instances can connect concurrently

column1: Can EBS volumes be mounted to multiple instances simultaneously?
column2: No, EBS volumes can only be mounted to a single instance at a time

column1: Can S3 be mounted directly to an EC2 instance?
column2: No, S3 provides web connectivity but cannot be mounted directly to an instance

column1: Is there any impact to EBS volume availability when taking a snapshot?
column2: No, there is no impact to volume availability when commencing a snapshot

column1: What can Amazon S3 bucket policies specify?
column2: Request IP range, AWS account, and prefix for accessible objects (not company name or country of origin)

column1: Is S3 appropriate for boot volumes?
column2: No, S3 is object storage; use EBS or instance store volumes for boot volumes

column1: Is S3 appropriate for primary database storage?
column2: No, S3 is object storage not transactional block storage; databases need block-based storage

column1: What S3 feature allows sharing objects with time-limited access?
column2: Presigned URLs allow optionally sharing objects with time-limited access

column1: What S3 feature enables managing access to buckets and objects by AWS accounts or groups?
column2: S3 Access Control Lists (ACLs) manage access and define which accounts/groups have access

column1: Do S3 lifecycle policies restrict data access?
column2: No, lifecycle policies manage object transitions and deletions but don't restrict access

column1: What is an S3 bucket policy?
column2: A resource-based IAM policy that grants permissions to S3 resources for other AWS accounts or IAM users

column1: What S3 features protect against accidental object deletion?
column2: Versioning (stores all versions) and MFA Delete (requires one-time code to delete objects)

column1: Does cross-region replication protect against object deletion?
column2: No, cross-region replication provides redundancy but doesn't protect against deletion

column1: What S3 feature tracks requests for access to your bucket?
column2: Access logging (provides details about requests, useful for security audits and understanding usage)

column1: What are the benefits of S3 cross-region replication?
column2: Replicate data between distant regions, minimize latency by keeping copies closer to users, maintain copies in multiple regions

column1: Does adding a second S3 region significantly increase durability?
column2: No, S3 is designed for 11 nines of durability in a single region; a second region doesn't significantly increase durability

column1: What type of encryption must be used if data must be encrypted before being sent to S3?
column2: Client-side encryption (server-side encryption only encrypts data at rest in S3)

column1: How is S3 data automatically replicated?
column2: Automatically replicated across at least three availability zones within a single region

column1: Is S3 versioning required for data durability?
column2: No, versioning is optional; data in S3 is durable regardless of versioning being enabled
